metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the conditional brainstorm trigger with an unconditional read (matching the existing plan-review-gate pattern), or define a concrete, evaluable trigger condition (e.g., 'when any acceptance criterion mentions a test, an oracle, or a behavioral assertion').
findings.finding: The rebar-brainstorm integration uses a conditional trigger ('when recording tickets that include testing work, read the shared file') whose evaluation is left entirely to the LLM agent's judgment at runtime, with no defined trigger guard. Brainstorm's role is to clarify intent *before* knowing the ticket shape, so the agent may routinely not identify 'testing work' until after the file-read window has passed.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the brainstorm integration to: 'Before recording any ticket whose acceptance criteria will include or reference testing, verification, or behavioral oracles, read the shared file. When in doubt, read it — the cost is a file read, and the benefit is correctly-shaped ACs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rationale in the plan (or the README update section) for why the shared/ directory pattern is preferable to per-skill file copies, given the established per-skill companion-file precedent and the acknowledged portability cost.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-skill shared/ directory convention (examples/claude-skills/shared/test-design.md loaded via ../shared/test-design.md) without justifying it against the established per-skill companion-file pattern (e.g., rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md). The existing approach keeps each skill self-contained; the new shared/ pattern creates a cross-directory coupling that breaks skill portability (acknowledged in the README AC but not justified as preferable to duplication or to a per-skill copy).
findings.location: Plan 'Skill integration' section; AC line 'examples/claude-skills/README.md documents the shared/ convention'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the plan's 'Skill integration' or 'What' section, e.g.: 'The shared/ pattern is chosen over per-skill copies because [reason — e.g., a single authoritative source avoids drift across three near-identical copies]; the portability cost is mitigated by the README notice and by the file being small enough to copy manually.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the LLM-surface RED-artifact requirements between `test-design.md` and the existing `rebar-debug/SKILL.md` Step 4 / `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` Phase 2 — either update Step 4's LLM-surface paragraph to cite and apply test-design.md's stricter multi-sample/threshold/pinning requirements, or explicitly state which document governs for each sub-case.
findings.finding: The plan introduces LLM-surface eval requirements in test-design.md (pinned model/settings, paired before/after prompts, multiple samples with predeclared threshold, negative control, quantified failure rates) that conflict with the existing LLM-surface RED-artifact protocol in rebar-debug's Step 4 and llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 (which only requires 'an eval or behavioural assertion that fails against the current instruction'). The plan never states whether test-design.md supersedes, augments, or is reconciled with the existing llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 spec — leaving two co-resident specifications with divergent requirements for what constitutes a valid LLM-surface RED artifact.
findings.location: Plan section: 'LLM-surface evals' bullet; rebar-debug SKILL.md Step 4 (line 444–446); examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2 (lines 112–114)
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's rebar-debug integration section, add: 'Step 4's LLM-surface paragraph is updated to cite test-design.md's LLM-surface eval requirements (pinned model, multiple samples, predeclared threshold, negative control) as the governing RED-artifact standard, superseding the single-eval description currently in that paragraph and in llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md Phase 2.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact section or insertion anchor in rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md where the shared file reference should appear (e.g. 'add to the existing "Before recording tickets" section at line 124').
findings.finding: The rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md integration point is not precisely located. The plan says 'when recording tickets that include testing work, read the shared file so acceptance criteria name the tier and observable oracle' but does not identify which section of the brainstorm SKILL.md receives the reference. The file has a directly relevant section — 'Before recording tickets, read the project's plan-review gate' (line 124) — that is the obvious anchor, but the plan leaves the insertion point ambiguous, making it impossible to verify the AC ('All three SKILL.md files reference the shared file at the integration points named above') against a specific location.
findings.location: Plan section 'Skill integration' — rebar-brainstorm bullet; AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Skill integration' bullet for rebar-brainstorm, name the target section: 'in the existing "Before recording tickets" section, add a step to read ../shared/test-design.md so acceptance criteria name the tier and observable oracle.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rationale in the plan for why a cross-skill `shared/` directory is chosen over the established per-skill reference-file pattern (e.g., 'identical content would otherwise diverge across three maintained copies'), and confirm the README copy-portability note accounts for this dependency structurally.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new cross-skill `shared/` directory convention with no precedent in `examples/claude-skills/` — the established pattern is strictly per-skill supplementary files (rebar-debug uses `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` inside its own dir; rebar-janitor uses `references/` and `phases/` inside its own dir). The plan asserts the new pattern without affirmative justification for why the per-skill pattern (e.g., a `test-design.md` copied into each skill's directory) is insufficient, making the abstraction harder to evaluate against the premature-abstraction bar.
findings.location: Skill integration section / `examples/claude-skills/shared/` (new directory); contrasted with existing `examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` and `examples/claude-skills/rebar-janitor/references/`
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the plan's 'Skill integration' or 'What' section: 'A shared/ directory is used rather than per-skill copies because the content is identical across all three skills and per-skill copies would diverge; the README copy note is updated to require copying shared/ alongside any individual skill.' This transforms the structural choice from asserted to justified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the co-location rule for the contract card in the Step 5 fixer handoff — state whether it precedes, follows, or is interleaved with the confirmed root cause / evidence / reproduction steps, and note any emphasis marker (e.g., bold header, XML tag) to prevent positional-bias de-emphasis.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the rebar-debug fixer subagent 'additionally receives the frozen contract card (intended postcondition + invariants)' but does not specify where in the Step 5 prompt/handoff this new artifact is co-located. Step 5's current handoff structure is fully specified ('confirmed root cause, the evidence, and the original reproduction steps') and the plan does not say whether the contract card prepends, appends, or replaces a portion of that context — leaving instruction placement ambiguous for the executor authoring the new Step 5 text.
findings.location: Plan section: 'Skill integration: rebar-debug/SKILL.md Step 5'; rebar-debug SKILL.md Step 5 lines 455–461

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback protocol for the rebar-brainstorm tier-selection integration: specify what the agent should write in the AC when the mechanism is not yet known at brainstorm time (e.g., 'record the expected tier as a provisional label with a stated rationale, and mark it as subject to revision during Phase 1 confirmation').
findings.finding: The rebar-brainstorm integration specifies that when recording tickets with testing work, the agent should 'read the shared file so acceptance criteria name the tier and observable oracle' — but provides no fallback protocol for when a ticket's testing work spans multiple tiers or cannot be unambiguously mapped to a single entry in the bug-class→tier table. The plan's tier-selection rule ('smallest tier that still crosses every component in the proven mechanism') requires a known mechanism, but brainstorm-phase tickets are written before any mechanism is proven.
findings.location: Plan section: 'Skill integration: rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md'; Plan bullet: 'Test-tier selection rule'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that Claude Code skills support cross-directory relative-path file references (e.g. `../shared/test-design.md`) by checking Claude Code skills documentation or testing the pattern; if not supported, adopt an alternative mechanism (absolute path, same-directory copy/symlink, or inline inclusion) and update the plan accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'each loads the shared file relative to the skill dir, ../shared/test-design.md' as if cross-directory relative-path referencing of companion markdown files is an established pattern in these skills. Probing all three SKILL.md files and the README finds zero cross-directory file references; the only comparable pattern (rebar-debug referencing 'llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md') is a same-directory sibling. The '../shared/' path crosses into a parent directory — there is no evidence this referencing style works or is used anywhere in the skill set.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Skill integration (each loads the shared file relative to the skill dir, ../shared/test-design.md)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or supplement the `make lint` AC with a gate that actually covers the deliverables — e.g. a manual review checklist item, a Markdown linter, or at minimum note that `make lint` does not cover files in `examples/` so reviewers know to verify content manually.
findings.finding: The `make lint` AC is vacuously satisfied for the new Markdown file. The Makefile's `lint` target runs `ruff check src tests` and `ruff format --check src tests` — it does not cover files in `examples/`. A malformed or empty `examples/claude-skills/shared/test-design.md` will not cause `make lint` to fail, so the AC provides no content-quality gate for the new shared file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and document in the plan) how Claude Code skill runners resolve relative file paths from SKILL.md, and confirm that a ../shared/test-design.md reference will be read correctly; adjust the loading instruction format if same-directory or absolute-from-skills-root references are needed instead.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that SKILL.md files will 'load the shared file relative to the skill dir, ../shared/test-design.md', but this is an unverified capability claim about how Claude Code resolves relative file paths from within a SKILL.md context. The existing companion-file pattern (same-directory references like 'llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md in this skill's directory') works because the LLM is instructed to read a file by name in a known location; cross-directory relative paths (../) may not resolve the same way depending on the Claude Code skill runner's working directory.
findings.location: Plan 'Skill integration' section: 'each loads the shared file relative to the skill dir, ../shared/test-design.md'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the plan body's rebar-implement integration description, name Phase 4a/4b explicitly (e.g., quote the phase heading or describe what each phase does) so the integration point is self-contained and AC line 2 is independently verifiable.
findings.finding: AC line 2 verifies rebar-implement integration by referencing 'the integration points named above', which in the plan body is 'Phase 4a/4b reference the shared file for oracle design.' Neither the plan body nor the AC defines what Phase 4a/4b are, so an implementer who does not already know the rebar-implement SKILL.md structure cannot verify this criterion — the criterion measures an entity (Phase 4a/4b) not defined in the plan.
findings.location: Skill integration section — rebar-implement paragraph; AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical after 'Phase 4a/4b': e.g., 'Phase 4a (test design) and Phase 4b (oracle proof) — the phases in rebar-implement/SKILL.md that cover authoring and validating tests — reference the shared file for oracle design …'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define update ownership for `shared/test-design.md`: name who is responsible for reviewing the three SKILL.md files for consistency when the shared file changes, and add a note in the shared file or README describing this protocol.
findings.finding: The plan defines who creates `shared/test-design.md` and who consumes it, but specifies no ownership or update protocol for when the shared file evolves (e.g., new bug class added to the tier table). If one contributor updates the shared file to add a new tier or change the fault-realism rule, there is no guidance on whether/how the three SKILL.md files need to be reviewed for consistency, nor who is responsible. This is a lifecycle gap: UPDATE ownership is undefined.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Maintenance' section to `examples/claude-skills/README.md` (or as a front-matter note in `shared/test-design.md`) stating: 'Changes to this file should be accompanied by a review of each referencing SKILL.md to ensure inline summaries remain consistent. The shared file is the authoritative source; SKILL.md files may summarize but must not contradict it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale in the plan for the shared-file approach (e.g., 'a single source of truth ensures consistent guidance across all skills and eliminates triple-maintenance; the shared/ convention is documented in README.md so deployers know to copy it alongside any skill').
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for the chosen 'shared reference file' approach over the obvious alternative (inlining content directly into each SKILL.md). The README states skills are used by copying a single skill directory, making the shared-file approach a usability tradeoff that deserves explicit justification.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section (top-level description); AC3 ('README.md documents the shared/ convention')
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'What' section: 'A shared file, rather than three inlined copies, ensures updates propagate to all skills from a single edit; the README.md convention documents that deployers must copy `shared/` alongside any individual skill directory.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
